Glossary About
Early Modern

Anne Conway

1631 – 1679

Spirit, vitality, and a living continuum between mind and nature.

A small correction at the outset: Anne Conway was a woman, so I will refer to her as “she” and “her.” The deeper point is that Conway’s philosophy of mind cannot be extracted by isolating “mind” from her metaphysics, theology, and theory of nature. In her system, the soul is not a detachable immaterial substance in the Cartesian sense; mind belongs to a graded, living, spiritual-corporeal cosmos whose order includes God, Christ or “Middle Nature,” angels, humans, animals, plants, minerals, and even apparently inert matter. God and angels are therefore not marginal theological ornaments. They are structurally important: God is the archetype and source of life, intelligence, goodness, and agency; Christ is the metaphysical-cognitive mediator between God and creatures; angels are higher or subtler instances of created mentality and, in one of Conway’s more unusual claims, also name ministering spirits internal or proximate to human psychic life.

The most defensible reconstruction is this: Conway’s philosophy of mind is a theistic, graded, embodied, vitalist panpsychism organized by a composite-subject theory of finite minds and a teleological moral psychology of transformation. It rejects Cartesian mind-body dualism, Hobbesian reduction of mind to local mechanical motion, and Spinozistic identification of God and creatures. It also rejects dead matter. Every created thing has spirit and body, not as two separate substances but as modes or degrees of one created spiritual substance. Finite mind is always embodied, always internally plural, always capable of transformation, and always placed within a moral order of purification, ascent, descent, punishment, and restoration.

1. Textual and historical orientation

Conway’s principal philosophical work is The Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy, first published posthumously in Latin in 1690 and in English in 1692. The text presents itself as a treatment “concerning God, Christ, and the Creature; that is, concerning Spirit, and Matter in General,” which is already a warning against extracting “mind” from theological metaphysics too quickly. Modern reference sources summarize the treatise as a late-seventeenth-century critique of Descartes, Henry More, Hobbes, and Spinoza, while also noting Conway’s affinities with Platonism, Kabbalism, van Helmontian medicine, Quakerism, and later Leibnizian themes.

Conway’s philosophy is built around a threefold hierarchy: God, Christ or Middle Nature, and Creature. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes this as a tripartite hierarchy in which God is the source of all things, Christ or Middle Nature bridges God and creatures, and created being is one kind of spiritual substance appearing in many degrees. Conway’s own text states that God is “Spirit, Light, and Life,” infinitely wise, good, just, omniscient, omnipresent, and omnipotent; it also says that God is distinct from creatures but not separated from them, since God is intimately present to all creatures as the giver of being, life, body, and good.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Conway does not start, as Descartes does, with an isolated thinking ego. She starts with a divine source of life and intelligence, then asks what created life and created intelligence must be like if they derive from such a source. Her psychology is therefore metaphysical from the beginning.

2. The central anti-dualist thesis: soul and body differ by degree, not by substance

The core of Conway’s philosophy of mind is her rejection of a real substantial divide between soul and body. She writes that every creature is composed of body and spirit, and that every created thing is changeable; if anything created were immutable, it would be God. Later she makes the claim still more explicit: every spirit has its body and every body has its spirit; every human being and every creature consists of many spirits and bodies; every body is spirit, and the distinction between body and spirit is only modal and gradual, not essential or substantial.

This is the decisive move. Conway does not say, “There are minds and there are bodies, and we must explain how they interact.” She says, in effect: the supposed gap was wrongly drawn. Body is not dead extension. Spirit is not wholly bodiless. The finite soul is embodied because all finite spirit has bodily expression, and body is ensouled because all finite body has spirit, life, and the capacity for transformation.

The result is not ordinary materialism. Conway attacks the view that nature is a dead mechanism. She explicitly opposes the Cartesian claim that body is a dead mass and says that nature is a living body with life and sense. She also rejects Hobbes’s and Spinoza’s systems as confusing, in different ways, the relation between God, creatures, matter, and mind. Nor is the result Cartesian immaterialism. Her position is closer to what contemporary scholars call hylomorphic or vitalist panpsychism: every creature has a body-forming, body-inhabiting, life-bearing spiritual constitution. Viljanen’s study characterizes Conway as a panpsychist because “nothing is nonmental” in her created world, while also noting that she preserves a threefold distinction among God, Christ, and creatures.

A good formula is: finite mind is spiritual body and bodily spirit. It is not reducible to gross matter, but it is never absolutely disembodied.

3. Why Conway rejects Cartesian dualism

Conway gives several arguments against a sharp mind-body dualism. They are not casual objections; together they form a coherent theory of embodied mentality.

First, she argues from divine creation. God is living, good, communicative, and wise; therefore God does not create dead things utterly devoid of life, knowledge, and spirit. Conway writes that God’s living goodness communicates life, power, love, and knowledge, and she denies that any created thing could partake of nothing of life or knowledge. The metaphysical premise is that created nature bears some likeness to its source. A dead, purely inert world would be theologically and metaphysically unintelligible.

Second, she argues from the soul’s love for the body. A soul loves its body because there is affinity or similitude between them; if body were a dead trunk, incapable of life and perfection, the union would be inexplicable. This is an important psychological argument. Love is not merely an emotion here; it is a sign of metaphysical likeness. The soul-body relation is possible because body and soul belong to a single continuum of created spirit.

Third, she argues from pain. Bodily pain is not merely an event in a machine observed by a soul from outside; the soul suffers bodily pain. Conway uses this passibility to argue that soul and body must be connected by shared nature. If the soul has some corporeity, and body has some spirituality, the soul’s suffering through the body becomes intelligible.

Fourth, she argues from mediation. Soul and gross body are not joined by a magical leap across an ontological abyss. Rather, middle bodies and spirits form chains or links between the central soul and the grosser body. This is one of Conway’s most fertile ideas for philosophy of mind: mind-body causation is possible because the mind-body relation is internally graded. There are subtler bodily spirits and grosser bodily spirits, not an absolute discontinuity.

Fifth, she argues against the Cartesian account of sense and cognition. Against Hobbes, she denies that sense and knowledge are merely reactions or local mechanical motions. Against Descartes, she denies that body is a dead mechanism externally manipulated by an immaterial mind. Sense and knowledge are vital actions, though they may use local motion as their instrument. In the case of vision, for example, Conway allows local motion—light enters the eye and stirs bodily processes—but insists that seeing itself is a vital action, not a merely mechanical collision.

A French-language study of Cavendish and Conway makes the same point from another angle: Conway’s critique of Henry More’s “vital congruity” challenges the coherence of saying that body is inert and yet somehow fit for union with soul. The French discussion emphasizes that Conway replaces rigid essential differences between body and spirit with differences of degree, especially against More’s opposition of impenetrable divisible body and penetrable indivisible spirit.

4. The finite mind as a composite living society

Conway’s “soul” is not a bare, simple, Cartesian ego. A finite mind is an organized multiplicity. She says that every creature is composed of many bodies and many spirits under one governing spirit. She also says that no creature is merely one single thing, because action and knowledge require multiplicity; creaturely knowledge needs variety and multitude.

This is one of the most important points for reconstructing her philosophy of mind. A mind is unified, but its unity is not numerical simplicity. It is a structured unity: a central, governing, or “captain” spirit coordinates many subordinate spirits and bodies. Conway says that the central spirit is itself manifold, and that the center is called a center because other spirits concur with it. In human beings this central unity is so great that it remains an entire, everlasting soul.

This gives Conway something like a composite-subject theory. A person is not an indivisible mental atom. A person is a hierarchically organized living society. The unity of the human mind comes from governance, concurrence, and continuity of the principal spirit. The subordinate spirits are not irrelevant machinery; they are participants in the person’s perception, memory, affect, bodily action, and moral condition.

There is a striking anticipation here of later “monadological” and organismic theories, though Conway’s system should not simply be read as proto-Leibniz. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Conway’s creatures are composite unities containing infinite creatures and undergoing radical transformations, while maintaining identity through a dominant spirit. But Conway’s formulation is more strongly embodied than Leibniz’s mature metaphysics. The Spanish translator and commentator Bernardino Orio de Miguel stresses this difference: for Conway, “the spirit is its body,” while Leibniz later tends toward the idea that spirit grounds or is incorporated in body; Orio presents Conway as more direct and less committed to Leibniz’s later distinctions.

5. Perception, mental images, and memory

Conway’s account of cognition is built on “images,” “spirits,” bodily retention, and vital action. She says that objects send images into us, and these images require distinct places, forms, and figures. This is why creaturely knowledge requires multiplicity. Perception is therefore not a purely intellectual act by a bodiless mind. It is the reception, organization, and vital activation of images in an embodied spiritual system.

Her account of memory is especially significant. Conway says that thoughts are “true creatures,” internal children, and that they have body and spirit. She also says that memory requires body, because the image or spirit of the remembered thing must be retained “in Some-Body,” including what she calls the seed of the brain. This is a remarkably strong embodiment thesis. Finite memory is not a purely immaterial archive. Remembering requires a bodily-spiritual substrate capable of retaining images.

A recent abstract by Costello notes that Conway’s account of memory intersects with her broader psychological and metaphysical project and may raise theological and soteriological questions. That is exactly right. If personal identity, moral responsibility, repentance, punishment, and restoration depend partly on memory, and if memory requires body, then the embodied character of mind becomes essential to salvation and moral transformation. Conway’s theory of memory cannot be treated as a peripheral psychological curiosity.

Her claim that thoughts are “true creatures” should be interpreted carefully. It does not mean that thoughts are independent persons in the ordinary sense. It means that mental acts or contents have ontological thickness: they are living, spiritual-bodily productions within the creaturely order. Thought is not an abstract, inert representation. It is a real vital formation.

6. Sense, knowledge, and vital action

Conway distinguishes vital action from mere local or mechanical motion. She does not deny that local motion occurs. She denies that local motion is sufficient to explain sensation, thought, and knowledge. In her account of seeing, light’s motion into the eye is a mechanical element, but vision itself is a vital action stirred through that motion. Sense and knowledge are motions or actions of life using local motion instrumentally.

This gives Conway a sophisticated anti-reductionism. She is not rejecting physiology; she is rejecting the reduction of physiology to dead mechanism. The body’s organs matter precisely because they are living organs. Food and drink become chyle, blood, and spirits; through spirits derived from blood we see, hear, smell, taste, feel, think, meditate, love, and hate. That claim is medically archaic by modern standards, but philosophically it is important: Conway sees mental life as dependent on the transformation and organization of bodily spirits.

Lascano’s work on Conway’s bodies emphasizes this point: Conway argues that all substances are spiritual, yet created substance still has body; bodies are important for change, interaction, and the storage of thoughts, memories, and knowledge. That is an excellent concise statement of Conway’s philosophy of finite mentality. Mind is not bodyless spirit; body is the condition of finite change, interaction, memory, and cognition.

7. Degrees of mentality: Conway’s panpsychism

Conway’s created world is mental or proto-mental all the way down, but in degrees. She says that body may become more spiritual without limit, whereas no creature can become more and more bodily without limit; evil, darkness, and bodily grossness have limits, while good, light, and spirit do not. She also says that body has life and sense actually or potentially, that the body is the grosser part and spirit the subtler part, and that no body lacks motion, life, and spirit.

This is why modern scholars often call her a panpsychist. Still, one should be careful. Conway does not necessarily say that every grain of dust has reflective consciousness, conceptual thought, or self-awareness. She says that every created thing has spirit, life, and at least the capacity for sense, knowledge, love, joy, fruition, power, virtue, and further perfection. Even dust and sand can, through transmutation, become capable of higher forms of life.

So the best formulation is not “everything is conscious” in a flat modern sense. It is: every created thing is a living spiritual-bodily being whose mental capacities differ by degree, organization, subtlety, and stage of transformation. Some beings have obscure, condensed, dark, or potential mentality; others have bright, active, organized, rational, angelic, or sanctified mentality.

Viljanen’s description of Conway as “hylomorphic panpsychist” is useful because it avoids two mistakes. It avoids Cartesian dualism, since soul and body are not separate substances. It also avoids crude materialism, since body is not dead extension but living, ensouled, form-bearing spirit.

8. Personal identity and the governing spirit

Conway’s theory of mind must explain how an individual remains the same through radical transformation. She allows for dramatic transmutations across forms of life, including descent and ascent through different bodily conditions. Yet she also wants moral responsibility to remain intact. Peter cannot simply become Paul in a way that erases accountability; divine justice requires continuity of the individual’s governing spirit. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Conway’s “captain” or dominant spirit preserves identity through changes, while divine justice prevents one individual’s punishment or reward from being wrongly assigned to another.

This yields a distinctive theory of personal identity: identity is not sameness of gross body, nor the bare persistence of a simple immaterial ego, nor psychological continuity alone in the modern Lockean sense. It is the persistence of an organized central spirit through transformations of its subordinate bodies and spirits. The mind is a stable hierarchy capable of changing its bodily mode without losing its moral identity.

This is crucial for her moral psychology. A person’s body expresses the condition of the governing spirit, and transformation of the spirit changes the body. Human life, animal life, angelic life, and lower forms of life are not sealed species compartments. They are stages or possible conditions in a morally ordered continuum.

9. Affect, desire, pain, and moral psychology

Conway’s philosophy of mind is affective as well as cognitive. Love, joy, fruition, suffering, hatred, and pain are not secondary ornaments added to cognition. They are basic modes of living spirit. She lists feeling, sense, knowledge, love, joy, fruition, power, and virtue among the noble attributes or capacities of created body or matter when properly understood as living spirit.

Pain has a special role. It is not merely a brute evil. Conway treats punishment and suffering as corrective and restorative. Evil and bodily darkness are limited; punishment serves to restore creatures toward goodness, and the creature is ultimately directed back toward the good. Lascano likewise emphasizes that Conway’s system combines creaturely freedom with an ultimate divine ordering toward restoration.

This means Conway’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from moral teleology. Mental life is not just perception and cognition; it is the creature’s whole trajectory of love, aversion, ignorance, purification, memory, pain, and restoration. The mind is not merely a representational system. It is a morally transformable living center.

10. Freedom, agency, and self-motion

Conway distinguishes divine freedom from creaturely freedom. God’s will is not arbitrary or indifferent; it is eternally ordered by divine goodness and wisdom. Lascano argues that Conway treats God as both free and necessary in the sense that God’s will is necessitated by goodness and wisdom, while creatures possess a kind of mutable imperfection that makes failure and evil possible.

Creaturely agency depends on mutability. A creature can become better or worse because it is not immutable like God. This mutability is not a defect only; it is also the condition of growth, repentance, correction, and perfection. The finite mind is thus a self-moving, self-transforming, but divinely ordered center of action.

Conway’s critique of mechanism matters here. If mind were merely local motion, agency would collapse into mechanical reaction. If soul were utterly immaterial and body utterly dead, agency would become unintelligible because soul-body causation would lack a shared medium. Conway’s solution is that finite agency is vital action within a living, graded, spiritual-bodily continuum.

11. The mind of God

Conway’s God should not be reduced to “a very large mind” in a modern psychological sense. God is infinite Spirit, Light, and Life. God has no temporal change, no composition, no division, no new knowledge, and no new will. Divine knowledge and will are eternal. Conway also says that in God there is an Idea or Word, the image of God, by which God knows Godself and all other things; and she connects divine Will or Spirit with the way creatures receive being and activity.

This is not a detachable theological aside. It defines the contrast between divine and creaturely mentality. Creaturely minds are receptive, image-dependent, body-dependent, mutable, and composite. God’s knowledge is non-receptive, eternal, self-originating, and archetypal. Created minds know through images, multiplicity, organs, spirits, memory, and transformation. God knows through the eternal divine Idea or Word.

The mind of God also grounds Conway’s panpsychism. Since God is living, intelligent, good, and communicative, creation cannot be a dead external product. Created beings receive finite participations of life, knowledge, love, and power. God’s mind is thus the metaphysical source of all creaturely mindedness, but without collapsing God into the creaturely world. Conway is very clear that God is distinct from creatures, even while intimately present to them.

A respectful treatment of Conway must preserve this double claim: God is not merely another mind in nature, but neither is God irrelevant to mind. God is the infinite living intelligence from which all finite intelligence derives its possibility.

12. Christ or Middle Nature as mediator of mind

Conway’s Christology is also philosophically structural. She treats Christ as the “Middle Nature” between God and creatures. Christ is not spatially between God and the world; rather, Christ is a medium in respect of nature. God operates in all things, but Conway says God uses this medium as an instrument or cooperator because it is nearer to creatures. She identifies Christ with the firstborn of all creatures, the one in whom all things consist, and distinguishes the divine Word in God from the Word expressed in the humanity of Christ.

For philosophy of mind, Christ is the condition of communion between finite mind and God. Conway says that the human soul can unite with God through Jesus Christ, the true medium between both; Christ and the soul can unite because of affinity or similitude. This is not an optional devotional overlay. It is an account of how finite, mutable, embodied minds can be elevated toward divine life without becoming God.

The Christological structure also protects Conway against two extremes. Against dualism, it affirms mediation, continuity, and transformability. Against pantheism, it preserves distinction: God, Christ/Middle Nature, and creatures are not simply identical. Modern scholarship frequently notes that Conway’s ontology is not Spinozistic monism. Gordon-Roth, for example, emphasizes that although Conway is monistic about created nature in some sense, she remains a “trialist” overall because God, Christ, and creatures are distinct.

13. Angels and angelic minds

Angels are important for Conway’s philosophy of mind because they show what higher created mentality can be. Since every created being has body and spirit, angels cannot be Cartesian disembodied minds. They are better understood as highly spiritual, subtle, luminous, living creatures with refined bodies. Conway explicitly allows that body can advance to the sense and knowledge found in brutes and to the perfections found in human beings or angels.

There are at least two relevant uses of “angelic” language in Conway. First, angels are higher created beings, good or evil, within the spiritual hierarchy of creation. Second, Conway speaks of “proper angels” or ministering spirits of a human being. In a striking passage, she says that through spirits derived from blood we see, hear, smell, taste, feel, think, meditate, love, and hate; these are the proper angels or ministering spirits of a human being, though other good and evil angels may also come to humans.

This should not be dismissed as merely quaint angelology. It extends Conway’s composite theory of mind. The human mind is not a single bare ego but a hierarchy of spirits, some of which minister to perception, thought, meditation, love, and hatred. Angelic language names the spiritual agency of the subtle internal powers by which human mental life operates. At the same time, the reference to good and evil angels preserves a broader moral-spiritual environment: the human mind is porous to higher and lower influences, not sealed inside a private Cartesian interior.

Thus angelic minds are philosophically significant in three ways. They mark a higher degree of created cognition; they clarify that no created mind is absolutely bodiless; and they help explain the internal plurality of human mental life.

14. Conway’s relation to contemporary philosophy of mind

Conway can be brought into contemporary philosophy of mind, but only with care. She is not offering a theory of “consciousness” in the post-Lockean or post-Kantian vocabulary. Her key terms are spirit, soul, life, light, sense, knowledge, body, motion, image, memory, love, and transformation. Still, several contemporary categories are useful.

She is anti-Cartesian: mind and body are not two independent substances.

She is anti-reductive: cognition is not merely mechanical local motion.

She is panpsychist or panprotopsychist: all created things have life, spirit, and at least capacities for sense and knowledge.

She is embodied: finite memory, perception, and cognition require body.

She is organismic: minds are organized living multiplicities under governing spirits.

She is teleological: mental life is ordered toward moral purification and perfection.

She is theistic: creaturely mentality depends on God’s eternal life and intelligence.

She is mediational: the finite mind’s highest union with God occurs through Christ as Middle Nature.

She is hierarchical and developmental: mental capacities differ in degree, subtlety, organization, and moral condition.

This combination is unusual. Conway is neither a standard dualist nor a standard materialist nor a standard idealist. A recent French article title, “Anne Conway: neither monist nor dualist,” captures the difficulty, and its notes indicate renewed Francophone interest in the Trinitarian, syncretic, and anti-dualist dimensions of her system.

15. Scholarly debates relevant to extracting a philosophy of mind

A major interpretive dispute concerns whether Conway is a monist, pluralist, or something in between about created substance. This matters because it affects the individuation of minds.

Gordon-Roth argues that commentators agree Conway is monistic about the created world in at least one sense: body and spirit are not distinct substances but modal or gradual differences within created substance. Yet Gordon-Roth also distinguishes type monism from existence monism and raises the question whether individual creatures are distinct substances or merely modes/aspects of one created substance. Thomas has proposed a priority-monist reading, according to which the whole created world is prior to its parts, while Grey argues for a pluralist interpretation in which individual creatures are distinct substances and the whole is not ontologically prior. Harmon’s 2025 discussion develops a Christocentric priority-monist interpretation as a serious possibility, though he treats it as controversial and textually difficult.

For philosophy of mind, the stakes are high. If Conway is an existence monist about creation, then finite minds may be modes or regions of one created substance. If she is a pluralist, then each mind is a distinct created substance with subordinate creatures nested within it. If she is a priority monist, then individual minds are real but ontologically dependent on the created whole. The text supports more than one pressure: it speaks of one created nature or substance, but also of many creatures, governing spirits, individual responsibility, and divine justice.

My own reconstruction would be cautious: Conway is clearly a type monist about created mind-body substance, since all creatures are of one spiritual-bodily kind and body/spirit differ by degree. She is not obviously a flat existence monist, because her moral psychology needs persistent individual governing spirits. The best philosophy-of-mind extraction should therefore treat finite minds as real individual centers within a single continuum of created spiritual substance.

16. Multilingual reception and interpretive enrichment

The Anglophone reception often emphasizes panpsychism, anti-Cartesianism, Leibnizian anticipation, and the monism/pluralism debate. Non-English discussions add useful nuance.

The French OpenEdition study mentioned above highlights Conway’s technical critique of Henry More: if body is inert and soul is living, their “vital congruity” is mysterious. This helps us see that Conway’s anti-dualism is not merely a vague preference for continuity; it is a direct attack on a specific seventeenth-century attempt to make soul-body union intelligible while preserving strong dualism.

Recent French-language scholarship also frames Conway through questions of individuality, body, and the inadequacy of simple monist/dualist labels. Nan Lin’s 2024 article, “The individual and the body in Anne Conway’s vitalist ontology,” appears in Revue de métaphysique et de morale and is situated among recent work on individuality and individuation in Conway. Pierrès’s 2025 French article title, “Anne Conway: neither monist nor dualist,” is especially apt for philosophy of mind because Conway’s body-soul theory destabilizes both categories.

The Spanish Orio de Miguel edition is philosophically valuable because it draws out the contrast between Conway and Leibniz. In Orio’s note, Conway is presented as saying, in effect, that spirit is corporeal and is its body, whereas Leibniz later says spirit is incorporated or grounds its body. This helps prevent an overly Leibnizian reading of Conway. Her finite minds are more deeply embodied than Leibnizian monads.

17. Extracted doctrine: Conway’s philosophy of mind in theses

Here is the most compact systematic extraction.

First, finite mind is not an immaterial substance opposed to body. It is the spiritual, active, living dimension of a created being whose body is the grosser mode of the same created nature.

Second, every finite mind is embodied. Body is necessary for finite perception, memory, interaction, transformation, and moral history.

Third, every body is ensouled or spiritual in some degree. There is no dead matter in the strict sense.

Fourth, mentality is graded. Lower beings have obscure or potential capacities for sense and knowledge; higher beings have more active, subtle, luminous, and organized forms of cognition.

Fifth, a finite mind is internally plural. It consists of many spirits and bodies organized under a governing or central spirit.

Sixth, personal identity is the persistence of this governing spirit and its ordered unity through bodily and spiritual transformations.

Seventh, perception is the vital reception and activation of images through living bodily organs and spirits. It includes mechanical motion but is not reducible to it.

Eighth, memory requires bodily-spiritual retention. Mental contents are not inert abstractions but real formations within the living creature.

Ninth, affect is fundamental. Love, pain, joy, hatred, and desire are not secondary to cognition; they express the creature’s spiritual condition and its relations of likeness or unlikeness.

Tenth, agency is vital self-motion within a moral cosmos. Creaturely freedom is possible because creatures are mutable; they can improve, decline, sin, suffer correction, and be restored.

Eleventh, divine mind is archetypal, eternal, non-receptive, and unchanging. God’s knowledge and will are not finite psychological events but the source of all created life and intelligence.

Twelfth, Christ or Middle Nature is the mediator through whom finite minds can be united with God without collapsing the distinction between God and creature.

Thirteenth, angels are higher created minds, not bodiless Cartesian intellects. They have refined spiritual-bodily being and indicate the upper range of created cognition.

Fourteenth, human mental life itself includes ministering spirits or “proper angels,” which should be read as part of Conway’s theory of the internal plurality and spiritual agency of the human mind.

Fifteenth, the whole system is anti-reductive: mind is not machine-motion, but neither is it an isolated immaterial ego. It is living, embodied, graded spirit.

18. Main tensions and research problems

Several problems remain open and should be treated as genuine interpretive questions rather than defects.

The first is the individuation problem. How exactly do individual minds remain distinct if all created beings belong to one created substance? Conway’s appeal to governing spirits and divine justice gives an answer, but the metaphysical status of individual creatures remains debated.

The second is the consciousness problem. Conway clearly attributes life, spirit, sense, and knowledge broadly throughout creation, but it is difficult to map this directly onto modern distinctions between consciousness, sentience, proto-consciousness, cognition, and intentionality.

The third is the memory problem. If memory requires body, how exactly does personal memory survive radical bodily transformation, punishment, death, or restoration? Conway’s subtle-body theory helps, but more work is needed.

The fourth is the angelic taxonomy problem. Conway’s references to angels include both higher independent creatures and “proper angels” or ministering spirits associated with human bodily-mental operations. These should be distinguished without forcing them into modern categories too quickly.

The fifth is the Christological-mediation problem. If finite mind reaches God through Christ as Middle Nature, then Conway’s philosophy of mind includes a theory of spiritual cognition or union that is not reducible to ordinary perception, memory, or reasoning. That theory deserves more focused study.

Final synthesis

Anne Conway’s philosophy of mind is best understood as an integrated metaphysics of living spirit. The soul is not an immaterial ghost in a machine. The body is not dead matter. Every creature is a spiritual-bodily being, every finite mind is embodied and internally plural, and every act of cognition is a vital action within a living hierarchy of spirits and bodies. Human minds are governing centers within complex societies of subordinate spirits; their perceptions, memories, loves, pains, and moral transformations are all bodily-spiritual events.

God, Christ, and angels are indispensable to this picture. God is the infinite, eternal, non-receptive source of life and intelligence. Christ is the mediating nature through whom finite minds can be elevated toward God. Angels are higher or subtler created minds and also, in Conway’s unusual internalized language, ministering spirits involved in human mental life. The resulting theory is neither Cartesian dualism nor reductive materialism. It is a theistic, embodied, graded panpsychism in which philosophy of mind, metaphysics, theology, and moral psychology are inseparable.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
